quotes.text: The verb to err and the noun error are sometimes used as synonyms for transgression (see Alma 31:9; 37:8; D&C 6:11; 2 Peter 3:17). However, in other passages these words are used in the sense of an incorrect or mistaken action or inference (see 1 Nephi 19:6; Daniel 6:4). In this sense, to make an error is to do something inappropriate or incorrect but to do so without evil intent.
quotes.speaker: Dallin H. Oaks
quotes.attr: Dallin H. Oaks, Pure in Heart, p.34

notes: his windows being open [not in vainglory, but that there might be no obstruction to his view of the direction in which Jerusalem]

quotes.text: Solomon, in his dedicatory prayer of the temple in Jerusalem, referred to the people's praying "toward the city which thou hast chosen, and toward the house that I have built for thy name" (1 Kings 8:44). The Prophet Joseph Smith once counseled the Twelve Apostles to "make yourselves acquainted with those men who like Daniel pray three times a day toward the House of the Lord" (History of the Church, 3:391). And President Wilford Woodruff, in the dedicatory prayer on the Salt Lake Temple, said: "Heavenly Father, when thy people shall not have the opportunity of entering this holy house to offer their supplications unto thee, and they are oppressed and in trouble, surrounded by difficulties or assailed by temptation, and shall turn their faces towards this thy holy house and ask thee for deliverance, for help, for thy power to be extended in their behalf, we beseech thee to look down from thy holy habitation in mercy and tender compassion upon them, and listen to their cries." These prophets do not suggest that the direction in which one faces when he prays has some mystical significance, but, rather, that it is an attitude of spiritual "facing." To face the temple, which is the temporal representation of the House of God, suggests that one turns his heart to the Lord and the covenants made in the temples to be more like him. President Woodruff clarified this point in what he said next: "Or when the children of thy people, in years to come, shall be separated, through any cause, from this place, and their hearts shall turn in remembrance of thy promises to this holy Temple, and they shall cry unto thee from the depths of their affliction and sorrow to extend relief and deliverance to them, we humbly entreat thee to turn thine ear in mercy to them; hearken to their cries, and grant unto them the blessings for which they ask.
quotes.speaker: James Talmage
quotes.attr: James Talmage, House of the Lord, p. 142

quotes.text: The idea of the temple as a house of prayer and fasting has roots in the ancient order of prayer in which God's name prevails (see 1 Kings 8:28-29). The orthodox Jewish prayer quorum or minyan is traceable at least as far back as the Babylonian captivity. And the book of Daniel (6:10) spoke of his praying toward the temple three times daily, a practice later encouraged by Joseph Smith.
quotes.speaker: Truman G. Madsen
quotes.source: The Temple in Antiquity: Ancient Records and Modern Perspectives
quotes.attr: Truman G. Madsen, ed., The Temple in Antiquity: Ancient Records and Modern Perspectives, p.11

quotes.text: Prayers were offered standing facing the Holy of Holies in Jerusalem, a practice mentioned several times in the Old Testament; for example, Daniel 6:10, where Daniel "went into his house; and his windows being open in his chamber toward Jerusalem, he kneeled upon his knees three times a day, and prayed, and gave thanks before his God, as he did aforetime.
quotes.attr: Stephen D. Ricks in Kent P. Jackson and Robert L. Millet, eds., Studies in Scripture, Vol. 5: The Gospels, p.203

notes: The king took this time to deliberate, thinking that after sunset Daniel would be spared till morning, and that meanwhile some way of escape would turn up.

quotes.text: In Daniel 6, we read of how jealous administrators attempted to frame and then to eliminate Daniel, who lived during and after the Babylonian exile of the Jews, by persuading the Persian King Darius (ca. 500 b.c.) to make it illegal for thirty days to pray to anyone other than the king himself. Daniel's failure to comply was reported to the king. "Then the king commanded, and they brought Daniel, and cast him into the den of lions. . . . And a stone was brought, and laid upon the mouth of the den; and the king sealed it with his own signet, and with the signet of his lords; that the purpose might not be changed concerning Daniel" (Daniel 6:16-17; emphasis added). From this description it appears that a wad of clay (or perhaps wax) was pressed along a portion of the juncture of the stone cover and the mouth of the lions' den. This clay wad was then impressed with signets to certify the authority of the act and to indicate whether or not the stone cover remained intact. Although a document was not involved in this instance, the use of the signets of the king and his officials in this manner illustrates again how seals were used to indicate the authority of an action and render that act tamper-resistant.
quotes.attr: Dana M. Pike in Thy People Shall Be My People and Thy God My God: The 22d Annual Sidney B. Sperry Symposium, p.104

quotes.text: When the backsliding Israelites worshipped Molech, was it right for them to obey the law decreeing that their children should be sacrificed in the fires of that pagan idol? When they were overpowered by the worshippers of Ashtoreth, should they submit to the decree commanding carnal intercourse with the temple prostitutes? Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego, Jewish captives in Babylon, defied Nebuchadnezzar and did not worship his image of gold; thereupon they were thrown into a fiery furnace from which they were saved by divine power. (Daniel 3.) Daniel was cast into a den of lions when he defied the decree of Darius by praying to the true God. (Daniel 6.) Peter and John, being commanded by the Great Sanhedrin "not to speak at all nor teach in the name of Jesus," defied these Jewish overlords. Their inspired decision: "Whether it be right in the sight of God to hearken unto you more than unto God, judge ye. For we cannot but speak the things which we have seen and heard." (Acts 4:18-20.)
quotes.speaker: Bruce R. Mc Conkie
quotes.source: A New Witness for the Articles of Faith
quotes.attr: Bruce R. Mc Conkie, A New Witness for the Articles of Faith, p.685

notes: The last clause, "or ever they came at the bottom of the den," could have been translated "before they came to the lower part of the den."
of Daniel: for he [is] the living God, and stedfast for ever, and his kingdom [that] which shall not be destroyed, and his dominion [shall be even] unto the end.

quotes.text: Not only did Daniel's service benefit the king; the faith Daniel had in the Lord affected an entire kingdom. The king sent forth a decree "that in every dominion of [the] kingdom men tremble and fear before the God of Daniel: for he is the living God" (Daniel 6:26). Great was the service of one righteous man, serving in the world of his captors. Great will be the results as we serve, each in our own personal way, in the world in which we live.
quotes.speaker: L. Tom Perry
quotes.attr: L. Tom Perry, Living with Enthusiasm, p.62

notes: these visions once at Ramos where Charles Thompson stuffed him like a cock-turkey with the prophecies of Daniel, and crammed it down Joseph's throat with his finger. (H.C pp. 349-354) It is clear that he emphasized above that John saw actual beasts in heaven, where the Old Testament prophets saw images of beasts. Joseph qualified this statement by saying later in the discourse that the beast coming out of the sea in Revelation 13 should be translated the image of a beast as he had referred to it in Daniel's vision. (H.C. pp. 349-354) Daniel continues speaking of a little horn appearing among the other ten horns he had just previously described speaking great things (vs. S). One cannot help but see the comparison to the beast in Revelation 13 which also had a mouth speaking great and blasphemous things (Rev. 13:5). Daniel and John at this point were describing the kingdoms of men which had become degenerated and beastlike in character controlled by Satan (see chapter headings Daniel 7, Rev. 13). A long period of time is represented by the little horn as in the next verse Daniel, the prophet, tells us of the Ancient of Days gathering (vs. 9). Most scholars are not sure who the Ancient of Days is but because the one like the Son of Man comes to him they feel it represents God. Also in verse 10, one finds that the judgment was set and the books opened, which the scholars see as the great white throne judgment of Revelation 20:11-15. At this point latter-day revelation removes the seal of ignorance by telling us that the Ancient of Days spoken of is Adam (see D&C 116:1). Joseph Smith also taught by revelation that Spring Hill, Daviess County, Missouri, should be named Adamondi-Ahman because it is there where Adam should visit his people as spoken by Daniel, the prophet (D&C 116:1). Daniel describes thousands upon thousands ministering unto Adam and ten thousand times ten thousand stood before him (vs. 10). The Lord has told us that three years previous to Adam's death there was a similar gathering of all the righteous into the valley of Adam-ondi-Ahman. The Lord appeared unto them and set Adam to be the head of all the multitudes and to be a prince forever (D&C 107:53-55). Therefore, the first gathering at Adam-ondi-Ahman is a type or shadow of the future gathering. Elder Mc Conkie put it this way, "All that happened at Adam-Ondi-Ahman in those early days was but a type and a shadow--a similitude, if you will -- of what shall happen at the same blessed place in last days when Adam and Christ and the residue of men who are righteous assemble again in solemn worship" (The Millennial Messiah, p. 580). At the future meeting at Adam-ondi-Ahman there will be a system of judges, that is why Daniel speaks of the judgment. Elder Mc Conkie again stated, "there will be a great hierarchy of Judges in that great day, of whom Adam, under Christ, will be the chief of all. But Christ himself, he alone, will judge the wicked. All this we have heretofore set forth; now we are seeing Adam sitting in his judicial capacity, and the scene is glorious indeed" (The Millennial Messiah, p. 584) Daniel speaks of the thousands that will be in attendance at the meeting. Elder Mc Conkie suggested that "We need not to suppose that all these things shall happen in one single meeting or at one single hour in time. It is proper to hold numerous meetings at a general conference, some for the instruction of leaders, others for edification of all the saints. In some, business is transacted; others are for worship and spiritual refreshment" (The Millennial Messiah p. 585). In verses 11-12, Daniel tells of the beast being destroyed by fire. The Lord also declared that, "the great and abominable church, which is the whore of all the earth shall be cast down by devouring fire according as it is spoken by the mouth of Ezekiel, the prophet." (D&C 29:21). The reason that the Son of Man comes to the Ancient of Days in verse 13 is because Adam holds the keys of salvation under the direction of the Holy One (D&C 78:16). Joseph Smith taught that even today whenever the gospel is sent, or revealed from Heaven, it is by Adam's authority (H.C. 3:386). Adam received these keys over the spirits of the human family in the creation (H.C. 3:386387). In verse 14, Christ is given dominion again over the earth during this special gathering. Joseph Fielding Smith expressed it this way, " Our Lord will then assume the reigns of government; directions will be given to the Priesthood; and he, whose right it is to rule will be installed officially by the voice of the priesthood there assembled (The Way to Perfection p. 29). Joseph Fielding Smith expanded this doctrine by saying, "until this Grand Council is held, Satan shall hold rule in the nations of the earth; but at that time thrones are to be cast down and man's rule shall come to an end ... then shall he give the government to the Saints of the most high ... this council shall precede the Coming of Jesus Christ as a thief in the night,

question. A literary criticism of Alma 36 reveals the beauty of the chiastic pattern found therein. A study of Judaism will help in understanding certain Old and New Testament scriptures. The doctrine of divine revelation however, should overwhelmingly takes precedent in understanding biblical passages. An example of the differences between the historicalcritical approach and those who accept divine revelation for interpretation can be found within the Book of Daniel. Roman Catholics, as well as other denominations since the days of the Vatican II council have accepted the historical-critical method for biblical criticism. Many of that tradition doubt whether Daniel was even a historical person since the only mention of him is in the Bible. Latter-day saints believing in divine revelation have other scriptures that confirm Daniel as being a historical person. (see D&C 116:1, and D&C 118:44) The Savior, himself, mentioned Daniel in his Olivet discourse (Matt. 24) Even so modern scholars would question whether Matthew was reporting the actual discourse of the Savior or simply relying on the earlier tradition of Daniel. The Historical-critical method also maintains that the author who wrote Daniel lived during the intertestemental period (approx. 167 B.C.), and certainly not during the exile period (600 B.C.) as others have stated. These Bible critics feel that all the prophecies and events mentioned in the Book of Daniel can be seen within the context of the Maccabean revolt. An example of the interpretation can be seen with the little horn mentioned in Daniel 7:8. The little horn would be interpreted as the power exhibited by Antonius Enpiphines IV. This approach to interpretation is sometimes called the preterist view which understands fulfillment of all events and prophecies to be during the life time of the individual authors. On the other hand, a person who believes in prophecy can see the little horn as a future earthly kingdom growing up after the Roman period continuing on to the present. Another example can be seen with the prophecy of the Abomination of Desolation (see Daniel 11:31). Preterists see this as having fulfillment only during the Maccabean revolt where the temple was desecrated. The Savior in the Olivet discourse used this prophecy of Daniel to describe the future Roman persecution during 70 A.D. under Titus, and also of another Abomination of Desolation before his second coming. (see Joseph Smith Matthew) This principle of dual or multiple fulfillment was also taught by the Savior when he came to the Nephites, and declared that Isaiah's word should be studied with great diligence (III Nephi 23:1). He continued by saying that Isaiah, "Spoke as touching all things concerning my people which are of the house of Israel; therefore it must needs be that he must speak also to the gentiles. And all things that he spake have been and shall be, even according to the words which he spake." (III Nephi 23:2-3) Daniel chapter seven is a fascinating section of prophecy that without divine revelation and modern scriptures for interpretation will forever remain a sealed book to modern scholarship. The following is an attempt to discover from the perspective of divine revelation the true meaning of the seventh chapter of Daniel.

INTRODUCTION Modern Biblical scholars interpret the Bible according to their own predisposition, although objectivity is often claimed. These scholars can be placed on a continuum ranging from a completely allegorical approach to a literal view of the fundamentalist. The approaches are numerous including textural, historical, grammatical, literary, form, tradition, and redaction criticism. Theologians often forget that their own personal religious backgrounds often color their exegesis of a particular text. The movement today is toward a more historical-critical approach to biblical interpretation. President Joseph Fielding Smith declared that many of this so called, "higher criticism is in reality destructive criticism" (Man, His Origin and Destiny p.490). Nephi saw that the Bible would go through the hands of the great and abominable church in which they would pervert the right ways of the Lord, that they might blind the eyes and harden the hearts of the children of men (see I Nephi 13:27). The historicity of many biblical events and people, including the creation, Adam and Eve, the universal flood and the baptism of Christ have all come under question concerning their actual validity during this century. The great blessing that Latter-day Saints enjoy is in the belief of divine revelation. In November of 1831, the Lord revealed that those ordained to the priesthood "shall speak as they are moved upon by the Holy Ghost ... and whatsoever they shall speak when moved upon by the Holy Ghost shall be scripture" (D&C 68:3-4). This process of producing scripture in 1831 has been the same since Adam spoke as he was moved upon by the Holy Ghost which produced scripture and genealogy records (see Moses 6:8). Peter declared the same principle when he said, "Prophecy came not in old time by the will of man but holy men of God spake to they were moved by the Holy Ghost." (2 Peter 1:21) Therefore, when scripture is viewed from this perspective of divine revelation it takes on an entirely new meaning. For example, many scholars holding to the historical critical method of interpretation cannot accept the fact that Isaiah could have known of a man named Cyrus before he was born. They declare that this is proof that a later author, second-Isaiah must have written those sections in Isaiah where Cyrus is mentioned. A person who understands revelation can accept the fact that Isaiah knew of Cyrus. However, it must also be stated that L.D.S. scholars who believe in divine revelation can still gain much by the use of the above approaches to biblical criticism. For example, it is important to understand the historical context of a particular passage of scripture in order to gain the full meaning of the scripture in

AN APPROACH TO DANIEL 7 Chapter seven is the beginning of the vision that Daniel had while in captivity. He had these visions and dreams while upon his bed (verse 1). Nephi when telling of Lehi's visions also used the rare bed motif (see I Nephi 1:7), which is another confirmation that Daniel was a contemporary of Lehi around 600 B.C. Daniel describes his vision (vs. 2-14) followed by a commentary (vs. 15-28). He was permitted to see a vision of earthly kingdoms coming out of the sea (vs. 3). It should be mentioned that for the people of the Old Testament the sea was a symbol of chaos, foreboding, or evil. John also used the symbol of the sea to describe the beast which he depicted in Revelations 13:1. Daniel described these earthly kingdoms like a lion (Babylon), a bear (Medes-Persia), a leopard (Greece), and a beast (Rome) (vs. 4-8). Joseph Smith interpreted these passages by saying, "There is a grand distinction between the actual meaning of the prophets and the present translation. The prophets do not declare that they saw a beast or beasts, but that they saw the image or figure of a beast. Daniel did not see an actual bear or a lion, but the images or figures of those beasts ... but John saw the actual beast in heaven, showing to John that beasts did actually exist there, and not to represent figures of things on the earth " (History of the Church, vol. 5, pp.349-354) Joseph Smith was not allowed to give us everything he wanted to about the relationship between Daniel's and John's visions. He even admitted that he only had taught more about

quotes.text: I suppose that no one will question the fact that in thus being true to himself and his God, Daniel demonstrated great faith and courage. Not all acts of courage bring spectacular rewards. But all of them do bring peace and contentment, just as cowardice, in the end, always brings regret and remorse. I know that from my own experience. I remember when I was a boy of fifteen and we had been expelled from Mexico in the revolution. My folks went to Los Angeles from El Paso, Texas. I got a job there among some Mormon-haters, and I didn't tell them that I was a Mormon. Some time after that, President Joseph F. Smith came to Los Angeles and had dinner with my parents. He put his hand on my head and said, "My boy, don't ever be ashamed that you are a Mormon." You know, I have worried all my days that I didn't have the courage to stand up to those ribald men. I remember another occasion when I was in Australia on a mission. I went up to visit the Jenolan Caves-very wonderful, spectacular caves. And as we walked through them, the guide said, "If some of you will get out and stand on that rock over there and sing a song, it will demonstrate the capacity of this cave." Well, the Spirit said to me, "Go over there and sing 'O, My Father.'" I hesitated, and the crowd walked on. I lost the opportunity. I never felt good about that. The only thing that ever made me feel the Lord had forgiven me was when I heard President Mc Kay say, "I was inspired one time to do a certain thing when I was in the mission field, and I didn't do it. I have always been sorry since." Then he said, "Never fail to respond to the whisperings of the Spirit. Live so you can receive it, and then have the courage to do as it instructs." Let us all resolve to develop to the courage to be true to ourselves and to our Maker in all things in our lives.
quotes.speaker: Marion G. Romney
quotes.attr: Marion G. Romney, Learning for the Eternities, p.189-190
